lessons.title: Hello World
lessons.concept_intro: print() is how Python talks to you. It displays text on the screen. Every programmer's first program is making the computer say 'Hello, World!'
lessons.analogy: Think of print() like a megaphone — you put text in, and it shouts it out to the screen.

exercise.goal: Make Python greet the world — and you!
rules: Use print() to display 'Hello, World!'
Use print() again to display 'My name is [your name]'
Use print() a third time to display 'I am learning Python!'
examples: When you run your program, it should print 3 lines
The first line should be exactly: Hello, World!
hints: print() needs parentheses with text inside quotes
Text goes inside quotes: print("Hello, World!")
exercise.starter_code: # Write your three print statements below:
exercise.test_calls:
# This exercise uses print(), so just run it and check the output!
    pass

vocabulary.definition: A built-in function that displays text on the screen
vocabulary.analogy: Like a megaphone for your program
A string of characters, like beads on a necklace

vocabulary.definition: A named action that does something (we'll learn to make our own later)
vocabulary.analogy: Like pressing a button — it does a specific job

lessons.title: Variables
lessons.concept_intro: A variable is a name that holds a value. Instead of typing the same thing over and over, you store it in a variable and use the name instead.
lessons.analogy: Variables are like labeled boxes. You put something in, write a name on the box, and later you can look inside by using the name.

exercise.goal: Create a personal info card using variables
rules: Create a variable 'name' with your name
Create a variable 'age' with your age
Create a variable 'city' with where you live
Print all three using f-strings: f"Name: {name}"
hints: A variable is created with: name = "Charles"
Use f-strings to mix text and variables: f"Name: {name}"
exercise.starter_code:
# Create your variables here:
name = 
age = 
city = 

# Print your info card:
exercise.test_calls:
# Run this to see your info card!
    pass

vocabulary.definition: A name that stores a value so you can use it later
vocabulary.analogy: A labeled box — put something in, use the label to find it

vocabulary.definition: Giving a value to a variable using =
vocabulary.analogy: Putting something into a labeled box

vocabulary.definition: A string that starts with f and can include {variables} inside it
vocabulary.analogy: A template with blanks that get filled in automatically

lessons.title: Strings
lessons.concept_intro: Strings are text — anything inside quotes. Python can do amazing things with strings: combine them, repeat them, check their length, and much more.
lessons.analogy: A string is literally a 'string' of characters — like letters on a clothesline, one after another.

exercise.goal: Build a username generator from first and last name
rules: Write a function make_username(first, last) that:
Takes first and last name as parameters
Returns the username as: first letter of first name + full last name, all lowercase
Example: make_username("Charles", "Gouilliard") → "cgouilliard"
hints: You can get the first character of a string with: name[0]

vocabulary.analogy: Gluing two pieces of text end to end

vocabulary.definition: The position of a character in a string (starts at 0!)
vocabulary.analogy: Like a seat number in a cinema — first seat is 0

vocabulary.definition: A method that converts all letters to lowercase

lessons.title: Numbers & Math
lessons.concept_intro: Python is great at math. It can add, subtract, multiply, divide, and more. There are two types of numbers: integers (whole numbers like 42) and floats (decimal numbers like 3.14).
lessons.analogy: Python is a calculator that never makes arithmetic mistakes — you just need to tell it what to calculate.

exercise.goal: Build a tip calculator for a restaurant bill
rules: Write a function calculate_tip(bill, tip_percent) that:
Calculates the tip amount
Returns the total (bill + tip) rounded to 2 decimal places
Tip percent is given as a whole number (e.g., 15 means 15%)

vocabulary.definition: A whole number with no decimal point: 1, 42, -7
vocabulary.analogy: Counting numbers — what you use to count apples

vocabulary.definition: A number with a decimal point: 3.14, 0.5, -2.7
vocabulary.analogy: Measuring numbers — what you use for weight or temperature

lessons.title: User Input
lessons.concept_intro: So far your programs just run and produce output. But real programs talk TO the user too! input() pauses the program, shows a message, and waits for the user to type something.
lessons.analogy: input() is like asking a question in a conversation — you ask, then wait for the answer before continuing.

rules: Ask the user for their name using input()
Ask the user for their birth year using input()
Calculate their age (use 2026 as current year)
Print a greeting: 'Hello [name]! You are [age] years old.'
Remember: input() always returns a string — you need to convert the year to a number!
examples: Hello Charles! You are 16 years old.
hints: input("Your question: ") shows the question and waits for typing
int() converts a string to a number: int("2010") → 2010

tests.name: test_uses_int
tests.description: Converts input to number with int()

vocabulary.definition: A function that asks the user to type something and returns what they typed as a string
vocabulary.analogy: Like passing someone a microphone and waiting for them to speak

vocabulary.definition: Changing data from one type to another, like string to number
vocabulary.analogy: Translating between languages — same meaning, different form

project.title: Mad Libs Generator
project.description: Build a fill-in-the-blank story game! Ask the user for different types of words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) and plug them into a funny story template.
steps: Create a story template with blanks (use variables for the blanks)
Ask the user for each blank using input() (e.g., 'Give me a noun: ')
Store each answer in a variable
Print the completed story using f-strings
Add a second story template for variety
bonus: Add 3+ different story templates and let the user pick one
Save the completed story to a .txt file
Let the user play again without restarting the program
